denial_pattern_sources: algo_time_delta: ProPublica + Capitol Forum Cigna Investigation 2023; Senate Finance Committee UHC Investigation 2024
incorrect_demographic_guideline: Commonwealth Fund 2023 Review of Prior Auth Denials
denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

We have completed our review of the request for Excision of endometriosis with specialist (out-of-network) related to Stage IV endometriosis (N80.4).

After clinical review using Cigna Medical Coverage Policies and MCG Care Guidelines, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: No gap exception; in-network surgeon available. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria for prior authorization determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Out-of-network provider used without gap exception.

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize Excision of endometriosis with specialist (out-of-network). If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

Cigna Health Care Management
clinical_context:
The 50-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Stage IV endometriosis (N80.4). He has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended Excision of endometriosis with specialist (out-of-network) as the next appropriate step.

Only specialist has 200+ excision cases; in-network lacks expertise. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on out of network no authorization without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.
